docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This repository contains the school's Artificial Intelligence (AI) governance framework.

It provides a central, version-controlled location for managing AI policies, guidance, risk assessments and governance decisions.

The repository exists to ensure that AI is adopted within the school in a way that is:

  • Safe.
  • Ethical.
  • Transparent.
  • Educationally valuable.
  • Aligned with relevant guidance and legislation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence is becoming increasingly important in education, employment and society.

The school recognises that AI provides opportunities to:

  • Enhance teaching and learning.
  • Improve accessibility.
  • Support creativity.
  • Increase efficiency.
  • Develop future digital skills.

However, AI must be introduced responsibly. The school must consider:

  • Data protection.
  • Safeguarding.
  • Academic integrity.
  • Equality.
  • Accuracy.
  • Security.

This repository provides the framework for managing those considerations.

docs.title: Policies
docs.text:
• AI Ethics Statement
  • AI Acceptable Use Policy
  • AI Assessment Policy

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text:
• Student AI Guidance
  • Staff AI Guidance

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence Governance Review Cycle
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner Oliver Caplan Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Internal Governance

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This document defines how the school's Artificial Intelligence (AI) governance framework is maintained, reviewed and improved over time.

AI technology develops rapidly. Regular review ensures that school policies remain:

  • Relevant.
  • Effective.
  • Aligned with current guidance.
  • Appropriate for students and staff.
  • Responsive to emerging risks and opportunities.

docs.title: 2. Governance Documents Covered
docs.text: The review process applies to:

docs.title: Policies
docs.text:
• AI Ethics Statement.
  • AI Acceptable Use Policy.
  • Assessment AI Policy.

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text:
• Student AI Guidance.
  • Staff AI Guidance.

docs.title: Governance Documents
docs.text:
• AI Tool Approval Process.
  • AI Risk Register.
  • AI Tool Register.
  • Decision Logs.

docs.title: 3. Review Frequency
docs.text:
Documents should be reviewed at least annually.

Additional reviews should take place when:

  • Government guidance changes.
  • New AI technologies become widely adopted.
  • Significant risks are identified.
  • School practices change.
  • Feedback indicates improvements are required.

docs.title: 4. Review Process
docs.text:
The review process follows:

```text Review scheduled | ▼ Evidence collected | ▼ Changes proposed | ▼ Governing body consultation | ▼ Approval process | ▼ Documents updated | ▼ Changes published

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence Tool Approval Process
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner Oliver Caplan Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Internal Governance

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This document defines the process for evaluating and approving Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools before they are used within the school.

The purpose of this process is to ensure that AI systems provide educational value while meeting appropriate standards for:

  • Safeguarding.
  • Data protection.
  • Security.
  • Accessibility.
  • Reliability.
  • Ethical use.

The school encourages innovation but recognises that new technologies must be introduced responsibly.

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
This process applies to all AI tools used for school-related purposes, including:

  • Teaching and learning tools.
  • Generative AI assistants.
  • AI marking or feedback systems.
  • Administrative AI tools.
  • Research and productivity tools.
  • AI-powered educational platforms.

This applies whether tools are:

  • Free.
  • Paid.
  • Trial versions.
  • Integrated into existing software.

docs.title: 3. Approval Requirement
docs.text:
Staff and students must not use unapproved AI tools for processing school information.

A tool does not require formal approval if it is used:

  • Personally.
  • Outside school systems.
  • Without school-related data.

However, users must still follow the school's AI Acceptable Use Policy.

docs.title: 4. Tool Review Process
docs.text:
All proposed AI tools should follow the following process:

```text Tool identified | ▼ Initial review | ▼ Risk assessment | ▼ Educational evaluation | ▼ Approval decision | ▼ Pilot period (if required) | ▼ Full adoption or rejection

docs.title: Staff Artificial Intelligence Guidance
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner Oliver Caplan Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Staff Guidance

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) provides opportunities to improve teaching, learning and administrative processes. When used responsibly, AI can support staff by reducing repetitive tasks, improving accessibility and enabling more personalised learning experiences.

However, AI systems do not replace professional expertise, subject knowledge or teacher judgement.

This guidance outlines how staff should use AI safely, effectively and ethically within the school.

docs.title: 2. Principles for Staff Use
docs.text: Staff using AI should follow these principles:

docs.title: Human judgement remains essential
docs.text:
AI-generated content must always be reviewed before use.

Staff remain responsible for:

  • Accuracy of information.
  • Suitability of resources.
  • Educational quality.
  • Compliance with school policies.

AI should support professional judgement, not replace it.

docs.title: Protect student information
docs.text:
Staff must protect confidential information when using AI tools.

Staff must not enter:

  • Student names.
  • Student performance data.
  • Medical information.
  • Safeguarding information.
  • Personal circumstances.
  • Confidential school documents.

into unauthorised AI systems.

docs.title: Use approved systems
docs.text:
Only school-approved AI tools should be used for processing school-related information.

Before using a new AI system, staff should consider:

  • Data protection requirements.
  • Security risks.
  • Educational value.
  • Accuracy and reliability.

docs.title: 3. Appropriate Uses of AI
docs.text: AI may support staff in areas including:

docs.title: Lesson Planning
docs.text:
AI may assist with:

  • Generating lesson ideas.
  • Creating lesson structures.
  • Suggesting activities.
  • Producing examples.
  • Adapting explanations for different abilities.

Staff should adapt all AI-generated materials to suit their students.

docs.title: Resource Creation
docs.text:
AI may help create:

  • Worksheets.
  • Revision materials.
  • Question banks.
  • Examples.
  • Classroom activities.
  • Differentiated resources.

All resources must be checked for:

  • Accuracy.
  • Curriculum alignment.
  • Appropriate difficulty.
  • Bias or unsuitable content.

docs.title: Supporting Inclusion
docs.text:
AI may assist with:

  • Simplifying explanations.
  • Creating alternative formats.
  • Supporting accessibility.
  • Producing additional learning materials.

AI should be used to improve access to learning while maintaining appropriate challenge.

docs.title: Administrative Support
docs.text:
AI may assist with:

  • Drafting emails.
  • Creating meeting summaries.
  • Organising information.
  • Producing templates.
  • Generating initial drafts.

Confidential information must not be included unless using an approved secure system.

docs.title: 4. Assessment and Feedback
docs.text:
AI may support assessment processes by:

  • Generating practice questions.
  • Suggesting feedback structures.
  • Helping identify common misconceptions.

However:

  • Teachers remain responsible for assessment decisions.
  • AI-generated feedback must be reviewed.
  • AI should not determine student outcomes independently.

Professional judgement must always be applied.

docs.title: 5. Using AI-Generated Content
docs.text: Before sharing AI-generated content, staff should check:

docs.title: Accuracy
docs.text:
Does the information appear correct?

Has it been verified using reliable sources?

docs.title: Bias
docs.text:
Could the content unfairly represent:

  • Individuals?
  • Groups?
  • Cultures?
  • Viewpoints?

docs.title: Suitability
docs.text:
Is the content appropriate for:

  • Student age?
  • Subject area?
  • Curriculum requirements?

docs.title: Originality
docs.text:
Does the content respect:

  • Copyright?
  • Intellectual property?
  • Existing educational materials?

docs.title: 6. AI and Safeguarding
docs.text:
Staff should remain aware that AI systems may generate:

  • Inaccurate advice.
  • Harmful content.
  • Inappropriate responses.
  • Misleading information.

Any safeguarding concerns arising from AI use should follow existing school safeguarding procedures.

AI should never replace appropriate safeguarding processes or professional intervention.

docs.title: 7. Supporting Student AI Literacy
docs.text:
Staff have an important role in helping students develop responsible AI skills.

Where appropriate, staff should encourage students to:

  • Question AI-generated information.
  • Verify sources.
  • Understand limitations.
  • Use AI ethically.
  • Reflect on their own learning.

Students should understand that effective AI use requires critical thinking.

docs.title: 8. AI in Different Subjects
docs.text:
Departments may develop subject-specific guidance.

Examples:

docs.title: Computer Science
docs.text:
AI may be used to explore:

  • Programming assistance.
  • Algorithm development.
  • AI systems and ethics.

Students should still demonstrate understanding of their own code and solutions.

docs.title: Humanities
docs.text:
AI may support:

  • Research exploration.
  • Discussion of viewpoints.
  • Argument development.

Students should evaluate accuracy and potential bias.

docs.title: Creative Subjects
docs.text:
AI may support:

  • Idea generation.
  • Experimentation.
  • Exploration of techniques.

Students should understand copyright and ownership considerations.

docs.title: 9. Reporting Concerns
docs.text:
Staff should report concerns regarding AI use, including:

  • Data breaches.
  • Unsafe AI outputs.
  • Inappropriate student use.
  • Potential academic misconduct.

Concerns should be reported through existing school procedures.

docs.title: 10. Professional Development
docs.text:
The school will support staff in developing AI literacy through:

  • Training sessions.
  • Guidance updates.
  • Sharing of effective practice.
  • Evaluation of emerging technologies.

Staff are encouraged to explore AI tools responsibly and share successful approaches.

docs.title: 11. Commitment
docs.text:
AI represents an opportunity to enhance education when combined with professional expertise.

The school encourages staff to experiment responsibly with AI while maintaining high standards of teaching, safeguarding, privacy and educational quality.

AI should make teachers more effective, not replace the knowledge, creativity and relationships that underpin excellent education.

docs.title: Student Artificial Intelligence Guidance
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner Oliver Caplan Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Student Guidance

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming an increasingly important part of education, employment and everyday life.

The school recognises that AI tools can provide valuable support for learning. Students are encouraged to explore and use AI responsibly, while understanding that AI is a tool to support learning rather than a replacement for personal effort, creativity and critical thinking.

This guidance explains how students should use AI safely and effectively.

docs.title: 2. What is Artificial Intelligence?
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence refers to computer systems that can perform tasks that usually require human intelligence.

Examples include:

  • Generating text.
  • Answering questions.
  • Producing images.
  • Writing or explaining code.
  • Analysing information.
  • Providing suggestions and feedback.

AI systems can be powerful, but they do not think or understand in the same way as humans.

docs.title: 3. Using AI for Learning
docs.text:
Students are encouraged to use AI to support their learning.

Appropriate uses include:

docs.title: Understanding Concepts
docs.text:
AI can help explain difficult topics by:

  • Providing alternative explanations.
  • Giving examples.
  • Breaking complex ideas into smaller steps.
  • Helping identify areas for further study.

docs.title: Revision Support
docs.text:
AI can be used to:

  • Generate practice questions.
  • Create revision summaries.
  • Test understanding.
  • Suggest revision strategies.

Students should always check that revision materials are accurate.

docs.title: Improving Work
docs.text:
AI may help students:

  • Review the structure of writing.
  • Identify weaknesses in arguments.
  • Suggest improvements.
  • Check clarity and organisation.

The final work must remain the student's own.

docs.title: Creativity and Exploration
docs.text:
AI can support:

  • Brainstorming ideas.
  • Planning projects.
  • Exploring different approaches.
  • Developing creative work.

Students should use AI as a starting point rather than allowing it to replace their own ideas.

docs.title: 4. Responsible AI Use
docs.text: When using AI, students should:

docs.title: Think critically
docs.text:
AI can make mistakes.

Always ask:

  • Is this information accurate?
  • Does this match what I have learned?
  • Can I verify this from another source?

docs.title: Be transparent
docs.text: If AI has contributed significantly to your work, follow your teacher's instructions about acknowledging its use.

docs.title: Protect information
docs.text:
Do not enter:

  • Passwords.
  • Personal information.
  • Details about other students.
  • Safeguarding information.
  • Confidential school information.

into AI systems.

docs.title: 5. AI and Homework
docs.text:
Teachers will provide guidance on whether AI may be used for specific tasks.

Before using AI for homework, students should consider:

  • Is AI allowed for this task?
  • Am I using AI to learn or just to complete the work?
  • Could I explain this work without AI assistance?

If the answer to the final question is no, the AI has probably replaced too much of the learning process.

docs.title: 6. AI and Assessments
docs.text:
Different assessments will have different rules.

Students must follow teacher instructions.

AI may be:

docs.title: Not allowed
docs.text:
For tasks designed to measure independent ability.

Examples:

  • Exams.
  • Controlled assessments.
  • Specific independent tasks.

docs.title: Allowed with acknowledgement
docs.text:
For tasks where AI can support learning.

Examples:

  • Planning.
  • Research support.
  • Feedback.

docs.title: Encouraged
docs.text:
For tasks where understanding AI is part of the learning objective.

Examples:

  • Computer science projects.
  • Digital literacy activities.

docs.title: 7. What AI Should Not Be Used For
docs.text:
Students must not use AI to:

  • Submit generated answers as their own.
  • Complete assessed work where AI is prohibited.
  • Create fake sources or references.
  • Copy another person's work.
  • Generate harmful or inappropriate content.
  • Impersonate another person.

docs.title: 8. Making Good AI Prompts
docs.text:
Good AI use requires good questions.

A useful prompt should include:

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Explain what you are trying to do.

Example:

I am a Year 12 student studying economics.

docs.title: Task
docs.text:
Explain what you need help with.

Example:

Explain the causes of inflation.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Specify what type of response would help.

Example:

Give an explanation suitable for A Level revision.

docs.title: 9. Checking AI Responses
docs.text:
Before using AI-generated information, check:

  • Accuracy.
  • Sources.
  • Date of information.
  • Potential bias.
  • Whether it applies to your situation.

Remember:

AI can produce confident answers that are incorrect.

docs.title: 10. Building Future Skills
docs.text:
Understanding AI is an important part of preparing for the future.

Students should aim to develop:

  • Digital literacy.
  • Critical thinking.
  • Creativity.
  • Ethical judgement.
  • Problem-solving skills.

The most valuable skill is not simply knowing how to use AI, but knowing when and how to use it effectively.

docs.title: 11. Support
docs.text:
Students who are unsure whether AI use is appropriate should speak to:

  • Their teacher.
  • Their subject leader.
  • The school's AI Governance Group.

The school encourages questions and responsible experimentation with AI.

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence Acceptable Use Policy
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner Oliver Caplan Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Public

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This policy defines the acceptable and unacceptable uses of Artificial Intelligence (AI) within the school.

The purpose of this policy is to ensure that AI technologies are used safely, responsibly and effectively while supporting innovation and preparing students for a future where AI is increasingly integrated into education and employment.

This policy should be read alongside:

  • AI Ethics Statement
  • Assessment AI Policy
  • Student AI Guidance
  • Staff AI Guidance
  • Data Protection Policy

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
This policy applies to:

  • All students
  • All teaching staff
  • All support staff
  • Any individual using AI systems through school facilities or for school-related activities

It applies to:

  • Generative AI tools
  • AI assistants
  • AI-powered educational platforms
  • AI tools used for research, writing, programming, design or administration

docs.title: 3. General Principles
docs.text:
AI may be used when it:

  • Supports learning and understanding.
  • Improves accessibility.
  • Encourages creativity and exploration.
  • Increases efficiency without reducing educational quality.
  • Is used transparently and responsibly.

AI must not be used to:

  • Avoid learning.
  • Misrepresent work.
  • Circumvent assessment requirements.
  • Create harm or deceive others.
  • Share confidential information.

docs.title: Learning Support
docs.text:
Examples include:

  • Asking for explanations of difficult concepts.
  • Generating examples for revision.
  • Creating practice questions.
  • Exploring alternative approaches to problems.
  • Receiving feedback on ideas or drafts.

docs.title: Creativity and Development
docs.text:
Examples include:

  • Brainstorming ideas.
  • Developing creative projects.
  • Exploring programming concepts.
  • Supporting design and planning.

docs.title: Organisation
docs.text:
Examples include:

  • Creating study plans.
  • Organising notes.
  • Improving personal productivity.

docs.title: 5. Student Unacceptable Use
docs.text:
Students must not:

  • Submit AI-generated work as their own without acknowledgement.
  • Use AI during assessments where it has been prohibited.
  • Ask AI to complete assessed work on their behalf.
  • Generate fake references, sources or evidence.
  • Use AI to impersonate another person.
  • Use AI to create harmful, offensive or inappropriate content.
  • Upload personal information about themselves or others into unauthorised AI systems.

docs.title: 6. Staff Acceptable Use
docs.text:
Staff may use AI to support:

  • Lesson planning.
  • Resource creation.
  • Differentiated teaching materials.
  • Administrative tasks.
  • Drafting communications.
  • Generating ideas for classroom activities.
  • Professional learning.

docs.title: 7. Staff Responsibilities
docs.text:
Staff using AI must:

  • Review all AI-generated content before use.
  • Ensure resources are accurate and suitable.
  • Consider potential bias or inaccuracies.
  • Protect confidential information.
  • Follow school-approved systems and procedures.

AI-generated content should never replace professional judgement.

docs.title: 8. Data Protection and Privacy
docs.text:
Users must not enter:

  • Student personal data.
  • Medical information.
  • Safeguarding information.
  • Confidential school documents.
  • Passwords or security information.

into AI systems unless explicitly authorised.

Only approved AI tools may be used for processing school-related information.

docs.title: 9. AI Accuracy and Reliability
docs.text:
Users must recognise that AI systems can:

  • Produce incorrect information.
  • Generate outdated information.
  • Reflect biases present in training data.
  • Create misleading outputs.

AI-generated information should be checked against reliable sources before being relied upon.

docs.title: 10. Monitoring and Review
docs.text:
The school may review AI usage to ensure compliance with:

  • School policies.
  • Data protection requirements.
  • Safeguarding responsibilities.
  • Educational standards.

This policy will be reviewed annually or when significant changes occur in AI technology, legislation or Department for Education guidance.

docs.title: 11. Commitment
docs.text:
The school encourages responsible experimentation with AI while maintaining high standards of integrity, safety and respect.

AI should be used as a tool to enhance learning, creativity and productivity while preserving the importance of independent thought and human judgement.

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence Assessment Policy
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner Oliver Caplan Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Public

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This policy defines how Artificial Intelligence (AI) may be used in student assessment.

The school recognises that AI technologies are becoming increasingly common in education and the workplace. Assessment practices should therefore encourage students to develop the ability to use AI responsibly while ensuring that submitted work accurately represents their own knowledge, understanding and skills.

The purpose of this policy is to maintain academic integrity while supporting modern approaches to learning.

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
This policy applies to:

  • Homework
  • Coursework
  • Controlled assessments
  • Projects
  • Presentations
  • Extended writing tasks
  • Any other assessed student work

It applies to all students across the school.

docs.title: 3.1 Student understanding must remain central
docs.text:
Assessment should measure the student's own:

  • Knowledge
  • Understanding
  • Reasoning
  • Creativity
  • Problem-solving ability
  • Ability to communicate ideas

AI may support learning but must not replace the student's own contribution.

docs.title: 3.2 AI use should be transparent
docs.text:
Where students use AI as part of their work, they should follow teacher guidance regarding acknowledgement.

Students may be required to record:

  • The AI tool used.
  • The purpose of its use.
  • The prompts or instructions provided.
  • How the output was modified or evaluated.

docs.title: 3.3 AI should support, not complete, assessed work
docs.text:
Appropriate AI use may include:

  • Explaining difficult concepts.
  • Suggesting possible approaches.
  • Providing feedback on drafts.
  • Helping identify areas for improvement.
  • Supporting revision.

AI use is inappropriate where it:

  • Produces the majority of submitted work.
  • Replaces independent thinking.
  • Generates answers that the student cannot explain.
  • Misrepresents the student's ability.

docs.title: 4. Categories of AI Use in Assessment
Category 1: AI prohibited
docs.text:
AI must not be used.

Examples:

  • Examinations.
  • Assessments explicitly completed under controlled conditions.
  • Tasks where independent performance is being measured.

docs.title: Category 2: AI permitted with acknowledgement
docs.text:
AI may be used as a support tool.

Examples:

  • Brainstorming ideas.
  • Improving structure.
  • Generating practice materials.
  • Receiving feedback.

Students must acknowledge their use where requested.

docs.title: Category 3: AI encouraged
docs.text:
AI may form part of the learning process.

Examples:

  • Computer science projects.
  • Research tasks.
  • Digital literacy activities.
  • Evaluating AI-generated information.

Students are expected to demonstrate critical judgement.

docs.title: 5. Student Responsibilities
docs.text:
Students must:

  • Follow teacher instructions regarding AI use.
  • Be honest about when AI has been used.
  • Ensure submitted work reflects their own understanding.
  • Verify information produced by AI.
  • Maintain records of AI assistance when required.

Students must not:

  • Submit AI-generated content as entirely their own.
  • Use AI to complete assessments where prohibited.
  • Create false evidence of learning.
  • Use AI to avoid developing required skills.

docs.title: 6. Teacher Responsibilities
docs.text:
Teachers should:

  • Provide clear expectations for AI use in assessments.
  • Consider how assessment design can promote genuine understanding.
  • Explain when AI is permitted or prohibited.
  • Consider the limitations of AI detection tools.
  • Focus on evidence of student understanding rather than relying solely on detection software.

docs.title: 7. AI Detection
docs.text:
The school recognises that AI detection technologies are not always reliable.

AI detection tools should not be used as the sole evidence of academic misconduct.

Concerns regarding AI use should be investigated through appropriate methods, including:

  • Discussion with the student.
  • Review of drafts and development history.
  • Comparison with previous work.
  • Evaluation of the student's understanding.

docs.title: 8. Coursework and Extended Projects
docs.text:
For coursework and extended projects, students may be required to maintain evidence of their development process.

This may include:

  • Draft versions.
  • Research notes.
  • Planning documents.
  • Reflections.
  • Records of AI assistance.

The purpose of this evidence is to demonstrate learning and authenticity.

docs.title: 9. Artificial Intelligence and Examinations
docs.text:
All examination regulations and awarding body requirements take priority over this policy.

Students must follow examination rules regarding technology and AI use.

AI must not be used in any examination environment unless explicitly permitted by the relevant awarding organisation.

docs.title: 10. Supporting AI Literacy
docs.text:
The school aims to develop students who can use AI effectively and responsibly.

Students should learn:

  • How AI systems generate outputs.
  • How to evaluate AI responses.
  • How to identify errors and bias.
  • How to use AI ethically.
  • When human judgement is required.

docs.title: 11. Review
docs.text:
This policy will be reviewed annually by the AI Governance Group.

Reviews will consider:

  • Changes in AI technology.
  • Department for Education guidance.
  • Examination board requirements.
  • Feedback from students and staff.

docs.title: 12. Commitment
docs.text:
The school believes that the future of education requires students to understand how to work alongside AI responsibly.

Assessment should not simply attempt to prevent AI use. Instead, it should develop students who can use technology ethically while demonstrating independent knowledge, creativity and critical thinking.

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence Ethics Statement
docs.text: Field Details Document Owner AI Governance Version 1.0 Status Draft Review Frequency Annual Classification Public

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This document defines the principles that guide the responsible adoption and use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) within the school.

The school recognises that AI technologies are rapidly changing education, employment and society. The purpose of this statement is to encourage innovation while ensuring that AI is used safely, ethically and in a way that supports the school's educational values.

The school does not seek to prohibit AI use. Instead, it aims to develop AI literacy, critical thinking and responsible digital citizenship among students and staff.

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
This statement applies to:

  • Students
  • Teaching staff
  • Administrative staff
  • Leadership teams
  • External partners using AI systems on behalf of the school

It applies to all AI technologies used for educational, administrative or operational purposes.

docs.title: 3. Definition of Artificial Intelligence
docs.text:
For the purpose of this statement, Artificial Intelligence refers to computer systems capable of performing tasks that traditionally require human intelligence.

This includes, but is not limited to:

  • Generative AI systems
  • AI-powered educational tools
  • Automated decision-support systems
  • Machine learning applications
  • AI assistants

docs.title: 4.1 AI should enhance education
docs.text:
AI should be used to improve learning experiences, accessibility, creativity and productivity.

AI should support teachers and students rather than replace human expertise, judgement or relationships.

docs.title: 4.2 Human oversight is essential
docs.text:
All significant decisions involving education, safeguarding or student outcomes must remain subject to human judgement.

AI-generated information must be reviewed before being relied upon.

docs.title: 4.3 Responsible innovation
docs.text:
The school encourages exploration of emerging technologies where they provide educational value.

Innovation should be balanced with consideration of:

  • Safety
  • Privacy
  • Accuracy
  • Equality
  • Educational impact

docs.title: 4.4 Academic integrity
docs.text:
AI must be used in a way that maintains honesty and fairness.

Students remain responsible for demonstrating their own knowledge, understanding and skills.

The use of AI to misrepresent work as entirely original is not acceptable.

docs.title: 4.5 Privacy and security
docs.text:
AI systems must be used in accordance with data protection requirements.

Users must not input confidential, personal or safeguarding information into unauthorised AI systems.

The school will assess privacy and security considerations before adopting new AI technologies.

docs.title: 4.6 Transparency
docs.text:
The school will promote openness regarding the use of AI.

Where AI contributes significantly to work, teaching materials or decision-making processes, this should be acknowledged where appropriate.

docs.title: 4.7 Fairness and accessibility
docs.text:
AI should be used to support inclusion and accessibility.

The school recognises that AI systems may contain bias or produce inaccurate information. Users must evaluate AI outputs critically.

docs.title: School Leadership
docs.text:
The leadership team is responsible for:

  • Establishing appropriate AI governance arrangements.
  • Ensuring compliance with relevant guidance and legislation.
  • Supporting responsible innovation.

docs.title: Staff
docs.text:
Staff are responsible for:

  • Using AI appropriately.
  • Checking AI-generated content for accuracy.
  • Protecting confidential information.
  • Modelling responsible AI behaviour.

docs.title: Students
docs.text:
Students are responsible for:

  • Using AI honestly.
  • Following classroom and assessment guidance.
  • Evaluating AI-generated information critically.
  • Respecting privacy and safeguarding requirements.

docs.title: 6. Commitment
docs.text:
The school believes that AI represents an important opportunity for improving education when used responsibly.

Our aim is to prepare students and staff for an increasingly AI-enabled world while protecting academic standards, privacy and the importance of human expertise.

AI should be viewed as a tool that strengthens education, not as a replacement for the creativity, judgement and relationships that define effective learning.
